meta.title: R232 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes SL R232 engine — M139 four-cylinder, M177 V8, and the 805bhp plug-in hybrid. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes SL R232 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned R232 engine typically costs £3,500–£16,000 supply only, depending on engine — the M139 four-cylinder sits at £3,500–£5,500, the M177 V8 at £6,000–£14,000, and the hybrid M177.880 at £9,000–£16,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on engine complexity.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes SL R232 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M177.980 V8 in the SL 55 and SL 63 is our top recommendation — it's a well-understood, proven engine with a documented failure pattern (mostly oil leaks, not catastrophic), and avoids the hybrid complexity of the SL 63 S E Performance and the electric turbo complexity of the SL 43. The SL 55 is the sweet spot.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes SL R232?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve cover oil leaks on the M177 V8 (hot‑vee heat accelerates gasket wear), electric turbocharger and 48V system issues on the M139 four-cylinder, and — on the SL 63 S E Performance — hybrid system complexity that brings twice the powertrain components and twice the potential failure points. The AMG Speedshift 9G transmission is generally robust.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes SL R232 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if you choose carefully and maintain proactively. The M177 V8 is a robust engine with well‑documented, manageable issues. The M139 four-cylinder is promising but its F1-derived electric turbo adds complexity. The plug‑in hybrid is reliable for what it is, but its complexity means higher running costs and fewer specialists — and it's the most expensive to maintain by a significant margin.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes SL R232 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright 'avoid', but the SL 43 (M139 four-cylinder) is the one we'd counsel caution on if you're buying for long-term ownership — the electric turbo and 48V system are unproven long-term, and a four-cylinder SL feels contrary to the model's heritage. The SL 63 S E Performance's hybrid complexity demands a serious maintenance budget.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my R232 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil leaks that leave puddles or smoke (M177), persistent 48V warning lights or loss of boost (M139 electric turbo), or a noticeable drop in electric range / hybrid system errors (M177.880) are the key warning signs. Have any of these checked immediately to avoid escalation.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes SL R232 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well‑kept £100,000+ R232, a £6,000–£14,000 M177 recon is only 6–14% of the car's value — well within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. Even the £9,000–£16,000 hybrid recon is only 9–16% against a £100,000+ example. These are high‑value cars; replacement almost always makes financial sense. Scrap is not a consideration for these premium vehicles.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes SL R232 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper servicing, the M177 V8 can comfortably exceed 150,000 miles — oil leaks are its limiting factor, not the bottom end. The M139's electric turbo is too new for definitive data, but early signs are mixed; the 48V system is the longevity question mark. The hybrid's battery will likely need attention before 100,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the R232 and the R231?

acceptedAnswer.text: The R232 (2022–present, this page) is the seventh-generation SL, developed entirely by AMG on the MSA platform with a fabric roof and 2+2 seating. The R231 (2012–2021) is the sixth-generation SL, with a retractable hardtop and a more traditional Mercedes-Benz character. The R232 is significantly more performance-focused.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes SL R232 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is stamped on the block — visible from below on the M177 (check behind the front wheel arch liner) and on the M139 near the starter motor. It's also listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation. Tell us your variant and we'll confirm it.

hero.h1: Mercedes SL R232 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the seventh-generation SL roadster — from the F1-derived M139 four-cylinder to the thunderous M177 V8 and the 816bhp plug-in hybrid flagship. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the first SL developed entirely by AMG. Backed by 880+ real UK Mercedes V8 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes SL R232 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the R232 roadster — from the electric-turbo four-cylinder to the V8 plug-in hybrid that redefines the SL.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: M177: 90% confidence — well-understood V8 with established failure patterns; 880 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries in 2025. M139: 70% confidence — newer engine with F1-derived electric turbo technology; limited long-term data. M177 PHEV: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier; hybrid system adds complexity.

overview.intro: The R232 is the seventh generation of the Mercedes-Benz SL-Class, presented on 28 October 2021 as the successor to the R231. For the first time, the SL was developed entirely by Mercedes-AMG, sharing its new MSA platform with the second-generation AMG GT. The R232 abandons the retractable hardtop of its predecessor in favour of a fabric roof and returns to a 2+2 seating layout. The range spans from the F1-derived M139 four-cylinder to the 805bhp plug-in hybrid V8.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-AMG MSA (Modular Sports Architecture) • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz SL R231 / Mercedes-AMG GT Roadster (R190) • Successor: — (in production) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (R232 is a standalone roadster; shares platform with second-generation AMG GT).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The M177 V8 engine family remains one of the most-requested Mercedes engines in our data, with enquiries concentrated on the SL 55 and SL 63 as these high-performance roadsters enter the used market.

items.quote: no diesel engine fitted to the R232.

The twin-turbo 4.0-litre V8 is the heart of the AMG SL — 577 bhp, 800 Nm of torque, and a soundtrack that justifies the premium. Valve cover oil leaks and cooling system issues are its known weak points, but the underlying engine is proven across the Mercedes range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the pure V8 SL experience without hybrid complexity.

items.quote: The F1-derived electric turbo four-cylinder is a technological marvel — but it's also the first four-cylinder SL since the 190 SL of the 1950s. The electric turbocharger and 48V system add complexity beyond a conventional engine.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise efficiency and accept the four-cylinder's character.

items.quote: The plug-in hybrid V8 combines the M177's complexity with a 150 kW electric motor and 6.1 kWh battery. Two powertrains mean two sets of failure points — and the highest replacement cost in the range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the ultimate SL and have the budget to maintain it.

items.quote: The entry-level V8 offers the same core engine as the SL 63, with slightly less power but the same character — and significantly lower acquisition and maintenance costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 performance without the flagship premium.

items.quote: Often overshadowed by the plug-in hybrid, the pure V8 SL 63 offers genuine supercar performance (3.6s to 60mph) with none of the hybrid system's weight or complexity — and a cleaner engine bay for maintenance.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the purest AMG SL experience.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve cover oil leaks, coolant system
Valve cover oil leaks, coolant system
rows.ownershipVerdict: Flagship V8 — more power, similar risk.
Most Complex — two powertrains, two risks.

economicsRule.title: The R232 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£14,000 M177 recon against a £100,000–£150,000 R232 sits at 5–15% of the car's value — comfortably within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. Even the £9,000–£16,000 plug-in hybrid recon is only 8–16% against a £100,000+ example. These are premium cars; replacement economics are rarely the deciding factor — but the complexity of the hybrid powertrain demands careful consideration.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R232 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: SL 43 (M139) — cheaper engine replacement, better fuel economy.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R232 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: SL 63 S E Performance — hybrid V8 complexity and battery replacement risk.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R232 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: SL 55 (M177.980) — V8 character without the hybrid premium.

problems.title: M177 V8 Valve Cover Oil Leaks
problems.rootCause: The hot-vee layout concentrates heat between the cylinder banks, accelerating gasket and seal degradation — valve cover oil leaks are the most common complaint, with oil seeping onto exhaust manifolds.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of oil smell or visible seepage around the valve covers, have the gaskets replaced immediately — an £800–£1,500 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £8,000+ replacement that follows if oil loss leads to bearing failure.

cta.label: Compare M177 engine prices for your R232 SL 63 →

problems.title: M139 Electric Turbocharger & 48V System Failures
problems.rootCause: The electric exhaust gas turbocharger is a world-first in production vehicles — but the 48V system that powers it and the integrated starter-generator (ISG) can fail, leading to warning lights, reduced power, and in some cases, complete system shutdown.
problems.recommendation: The electric turbo system is the defining risk of the M139 — regular inspection of the 48V system is essential, and a £800–£1,500 preventative battery replacement at 30k miles is cheap insurance against a £3,500+ full engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M139 engine prices for your R232 SL 43 →

problems.title: M177.880 Plug-in Hybrid System Complexity
problems.rootCause: Two powertrains — a 4.0-litre V8 at the front and a 150 kW electric motor at the rear — mean twice the systems to fail. The 6.1 kWh battery (4.8 kWh usable) is the single most expensive component; cooling system integration between combustion and electric drivetrains adds further failure points.
problems.recommendation: The SL 63 S E Performance is the most complex SL ever built — if you buy one, budget for specialist inspection of both powertrains before purchase, and accept that a full replacement could exceed the value of lesser cars entirely.

replacementCosts.h2: R232 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the R232 shares its MSA platform with the second-generation AMG GT, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are R232 SL roadster only (2022–present). The previous-generation R231 SL (2012–2021) is covered on its own Generation Page. The Mercedes-Maybach SL (introduced August 2024) is a separate variant not covered here.
